observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the MTO institutionalize and define the responsibilities of
personnel in the updating of the RPTAR and the preparation of the certified
delinquency list, including timelines for data compilation, review, approval and
submission to the Sanggunian and other Departments.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant –
➢ make an adjusting entry to recognize the prior period errors in the set-up of
RPT/SET Receivables in CYs 2021 to 2023;
Particulars Code Debit Credit
Deferred RPT 2-05-01-010 ₱136,118,487.46
RPT Receivable 1-03-01-020 ₱136,118,487.46
To adjust the ending balance of RPT receivables, as the set-up of the RPT
receivables should be recorded based on the assessment roll
Particulars Code Debit Credit
Deferred SET 2-05-01-020 136,033,520.13
SET Receivable 1-03-01-030 136,033,520.13
To adjust the ending balance of RPT receivables, as the set-up of the RPT
receivables should be recorded based on the assessment roll
➢ utilize the certified list to set up the required subsidiary ledgers and validate
the accuracy of the RPT/SET receivables presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant –
➢ prepare an adjusting entry to correct the erroneous entries recorded under
JEV Nos. 2024-11-2038 and 2024-12-2134, and to accurately reflect the proper
balance of the affected accounts in accordance with the correct sharing of SET
receivables:
Account

To correct the erroneous entries recorded under JEV Nos. 2024-11-2038 and 2024-12-

➢ disclose the prior period error as required by paragraph 54 of IPSAS 3.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) –
a. For GSO Inventory
• immediately provide and utilize appropriate shelving, racks, and
storage systems for all types of inventory, especially office and
janitorial supplies, to ensure orderly storage and efficient management.
• strengthen internal controls over the return of excess supplies by
establishing clear policies and procedures to ensure all returned items
are properly routed, recorded, and accounted for by the Supply
Officer.
b. For RHU Inventory
• prioritize the provision of adequate and suitable storage facilities for
drugs and medicines, ensuring controlled environments to preserve
their efficacy and prevent degradation.
c. For Inventory Committee
• promptly issue an updated EO or OO to officially constitute the Inventory
Committee, ensuring that the designated members are those actively
involved in conducting the physical counts.

We recommended that the GSO, (i) provide a copy of the List of Non-Existing
PPEs to the MAO for appropriate accounting action; and (ii) strictly follow the
disposition and derecognition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs as outlined
in Items 7 and 8 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that –
a. the MAO provide adjusting entry for the recognition land that meet the
definition of an asset under IPSAS:
Particulars Debit Credit
Land ₱58,174,920.00
Government Equity ₱58,174,920.00
To make an adjusting entry for the recognition land parcels in custody
of the MTO that meet the definition of an asset under IPSAS.
b. the MTO and MAO conduct a thorough inventory and validation of all
land titles and supporting documents in their custody to determine the
existence, location, and ownership status of each property.

We recommended that the GSO prepare and secure duly acknowledged PARs
for all PPE items listed in the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the –
a. On the DOH - Health and Emergency Allowance -
● HEA Focal Person submit the documentary requirements to DOH for the
remaining two-month claims of Lamao Barangay Health Station to process
the funding; and
● Municipal Accountant, Municipal Health Officer and the HEA Focal
Person, submit the original copy of the payrolls and supporting
attachments to the AT of the remaining HEA for Lamao Health Workers
once it is liquidated.
b. On the DSWD - Supplemental Feeding Program -
● Municipal Accountant, MSWDO and the corresponding focal person
submit comprehensive photo documentation (with proper labeling on the
DCC name, date, and daily menu) and complete attendance sheets (signed
by the daycare teacher, SPF focal person, and MSWDO) for all subsequent
feeding programs to the AT.
c. On the DOLE - DARBA Coconut and Cashew Products and subsequent
procurements -
● Municipal BAC and its Secretariat consider the procurement by lots for
various items with different natures to secure the most advantageous price
from legitimate bidders;
● Supply and Property Custodian, Inspector and PESO Manager submit
their official comments on the observed discrepancies in the specifications
of delivered items compared to requirements, as well as on the undelivered
items;
● PESO and the GSO resolve the issues of undelivered items/delivered items
below the required specifications and submit the listing and photo
documentation to the AT in order for us to make a decision in audit;
● PESO and other end-user units refrain from altering the specifications of
goods to be delivered without the knowledge, review, and/or approval of
the BAC, Procurement Section, and/or the General Services Office (GSO),
as applicable; and
● GSO, through the Supply and Property Custodian and the Inspectorate
Team, monitor the schedule of deliveries and report any delayed delivery
to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer for the enforcement
of liquidated damages.
d. Municipal Mayor/Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE) –
● install control procedures and strengthen the composition of the
Inspectorate Team by assigning an inspector knowledgeable in the
specifications of the goods delivered and including a representative from
the End-User/Implementing Section, to have a proper check and balance
on the quantity, specifications and quality of deliveries; and
● enforce the delivery of undelivered items and/or liquidated damages in
accordance with the Government Procurement Act.

We recommended that the:
a. Finance Committee, examine and plan the full budgeting and funding of
employees’ earned leave credits;
b. Municipal HRMO to regularly update the Municipal Accountant on the
computed earned leave credits using Section 3.1 of DBM-BC No. 2016-2 dated
March 29, 2016; and
c. Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) to realign the OPB - Monetization of
Vacation and Sick Leave Credits to TLB to comply with IPSAS 39.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the MTO (i) assign personnel
at the MTO who are well-equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the
examination of books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry out their mandates
efficiently; and (ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business
establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or receipts
used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No.
7160.

We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer attend the
BLGF’s Training-Workshop on EBA and Pertinent Records of Business owners to
enhance his skills and knowledge as the MT.

We recommended that the MTO (i) actively follow up with the DICT to
address the inconsistencies and inaccuracies in the e-BPLS to ensure that the system
accurately reflects tax liabilities, including computed interests and surcharges; and
(ii) promptly issue Notices of Delinquency to all identified delinquent business
establishments.

We further recommended that the BPLO reconcile their records with the
MTO to ensure accuracy in the identification of delinquent accounts and collected
payments.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the MTO, (i) collect business
taxes from outside contractors who engaged in business within the Municipality,
applying the situs of taxation; (ii) coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to identify
the contractors who have bunkhouses or other structures that may be considered as
project office as a basis for the imposition of business tax.

We recommended that the head of HRMO i) formally notify all existing
contractual employees, JOs, COS workers, and consultants on the required
submission of accomplishment reports; and ii) incorporate the submission of
accomplishment reports as a mandatory provision in all future contracts for JOs,
COS workers, contractual employees and consultants.

We further recommended that the MAO (i) verify the authenticity and
completeness of supporting documents, including digitalized DTRs by accessing the
relevant system, if applicable, as well as duly verified and accepted accomplishment
reports, prior to processing payments for JOs, COS workers, and consultants; (ii)
maintain digital copies of DTRs for their back-up and use; and (iii) submit monthly
digital copies to the AT for audit purposes.

We also recommended the MIS to establish a secure backup and archiving
system for all digital DTRs and other critical personnel records to prevent data loss,
considering the prior incident in 2019.

We recommended that the HRMO prioritize the filling of vacant positions to
ensure continuity, legal compliance and workforce stability.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and concerned project
engineers:
a. guarantee the completion of preparatory activities such as, but not limited
to, cost-benefit analysis, feasibility studies, market research, and detailed
engineering designs, regulatory clearances and right of way acquisitions
for civil works projects prior to the approval of the indicative APP by the
HOPE to prevent issues from arising during the implementation stage,
allowing issues to be addressed and resolved proactively before
procurement activities even begin;
b. make a reliable estimate of rainy/unworkable days that could impede
works at the site, based on PAGASA records and incorporate it into the
computed contract time/duration utilizing PERT/CPM for accurate
scheduling;
c. refrain from considering the short-lived localized thunderstorms or
ordinary unfavorable weather conditions in providing contract time
extensions; and
d. institute the necessary remedies provided under the RIRR of R.A. No.
9184, which include, but are not limited to:
• Imposition of liquidated damages for delayed projects;
• Forfeiture of performance security, or application of stipulation
number two listed in the Performance Securing Declaration, as
applicable;
• Rescission/termination of projects with negative slippage of 10 percent
or more, serving as the basis for the issuance of disqualification
and/or blacklisting of the contractors.

We recommended that the –
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer initiate the application for transfer of
certificate of title for Limay Public Cemetery (Bernabe) and Limay Public
Cemetery (SFDA) to the municipality once the DENR has granted the
special patent to the latter cemetery, to secure ownership and for proper
recording by the MAO;
b. Municipal Mayor and the HRMO clearly define the boundaries of duties
and responsibilities for the MPDC and MEO to prevent subsequent
problems, such as penalties, arising from project implementation without
necessary permits and clearances from regulatory agencies, and to
maintain effective checks and balances between the two offices;
c. Municipal Engineer guarantee the completion of preparatory activities
such as, but not limited to, cost-benefit analysis, feasibility studies, market
research, detailed engineering designs, regulatory clearances, and right-of-
way acquisitions for civil works projects prior to the approval of the
indicative APP by the HOPE, to prevent issues from arising during the
implementation stage, allowing them to be addressed and resolved
proactively before procurement activities even begin; and
d. Municipal Accountant reconcile the recorded disbursements for the public
cemeteries with the cost of projects implemented by the MPDC to establish
reliable amounts per cemetery; and
e. Municipal Mayor, Municipal Engineer and MPDC establish a clear
naming convention or a set of agreed-upon rules for naming infrastructure
projects to facilitate easier identification and management of data and
information.

We recommended that the MDRRMO include the list of PPAs to be funded
from the unexpended portion of the prior year’s LDRRMF in the preparation of
LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the GFPS –
a. include in its GPB for the incoming year a training program on the HGDG
and request the assistance of a gender analysis specialist/expert in the use
and/or administration of the HGDG;
b. include in the municipal budgeting process the application of the HGDG
tool in the identified PPAs, providing inputs in the preparation of the GPB
and GAD AR; and
c. prioritize the finalization and implementation of the three-year GAD
Agenda in the concurrent with the Local Chief Executive’s term of
leadership.

To further enhance the promotion of transparency and accountability, we
recommended that -
a. Management clearly defines the responsibility with respect to the transmittal
of SEF budget and submission of accountability reports to oversight bodies;
and
b. Municipal Accountant furnish the SEF Utilization Reports to the members of
the LSB, prior to the posting of the reports.

We reiterated the prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed,
through the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant, to exert all possible efforts to
reconcile the Municipality’s records with the GSIS to prevent (i) any legal matters or
impediments; and (ii) may posed risks of deduction to non-collection of benefits of
members/beneficiaries.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant to
extend effort in reconciling the balance with corresponding government office and
proceed to procedures necessary to fully remit the balances.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that Management a)
continue its compliance with laws, accounting, budgeting, and auditing rules and
regulations and act swiftly on noted deviations in audit to avoid audit suspensions and
disallowances; and b) take appropriate action geared towards the settlement of the
disallowances which are deemed final and executory in accordance with COA Revised
Rules in Settlement of Accounts.

The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant have implemented a quarterly reconciliation process of subsidiary ledgers and cash books.
Implemented.

The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant have implemented a quarterly reconciliation process of subsidiary ledgers and cash books.
Implemented.

To ensure accurate and convenient financial data utilization, a collection matrix, is employed using Microsoft Excel. This tool categorizes collections according to the accounts prescribed by the local government accounting system and the Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM).
Implemented.

Not implemented.
Results of validation through the cash examination conducted, it was revealed that there were cash advances given to the same AO despite non-liquidation of previous cash advances.

Not implemented.
Management failed to provide proof of communication with the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU), as indicated in the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI). Although the Audit Team formally communicated with ICFAU regarding these cash advances, no response has been received to date. Appropriate audit action will be taken upon the collection of the necessary information from authorities.

Excluding long-outstanding Confidential Fund balances, the liquidation of cash advances is consistently monitored by the incumbent Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant.
Implemented.

The MTO communicates with payees/beneficiaries via text messages. Records indicate that most staled checks pertain to financial assistance for indigents, with contact information sourced from Aid to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) forms for beneficiaries, and old records for institutional/company payees.
Implemented.

The MTO now regularly submits Appendix No. 38 Report of Checks Issued (RCI), prepared daily or as needed, and the RCI is then forwarded to the MAO for individual transaction recording in the journal, and for appropriately documenting cash restoration, expense reversals, or payable recordings.
Implemented.

The MAO’s personnel perform a three-way match, validating Official Receipts against the Abstract of Collections and Report of Collections and Deposits, all originating from the Municipal Treasurer's Office. Upon validation, the transactions are recorded and reconciled with the MTO.
Implemented.

In response to a COA recommendation, certified true copies of documents detailing a cash advance for the Central Luzon Regional Athletic Association (CLRAA) 2020 training food allowance (Disbursement No. 200-2002-139, dated February 24, 2020) have been submitted. These include the Journal Entry Voucher for Cash Advance, Check No. 5946, Consolidated Training Design, Liquidation Report, Journal Entry Voucher for Liquidation, and payrolls showing distribution to various schools.
Implemented.

Four checks (Nos. 21122, 21252, 21253, and 21254), issued in June 2022 as refunds for St. Francis Homes, became stale by December 2022. These checks were endorsed to the MAO for cash restoration, a process confirmed by the MTO's cashbook. Consequently, the MAO prepared Journal Entry No. 2022-12-1345 to restore ₱1,745,023.22 in cash for these and other stale checks as of December 2022. References include excerpts from the General Journal for Trust Fund and a list of stale checks.
Implemented.

observations.managementAction:
The PATAS linkage to the MTO is currently operational, though not yet at its full functionality. The provincial government continues to provide technical assistance and training to support the complete utilization of the system.

The task will be carried out by the currently employed personnel, with additional support from On-the-Job Trainees.

The MAO utilized the data provided by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and Municipal Assessor’s Office.

The reported year-end balance did not reconcile with the MTO records. This discrepancy is attributed to the absence of a complete and accurate taxpayer list, which consequently hinders reliable reporting of Real Property Tax (RPT)/Special Education Tax (SET) receivables and delinquent taxes.

observations.managementAction:
The Public Employment Service Office (PESO) together with Municipal Agriculture’s Office of Limay inspected the awarded motorized fishing boats from various barangay. The beneficiaries of this livelihood program received free repair service from the supplier last May 05, 2024.

On June 21, 2024, the Department of Labor and Employment and PESO Limay organized a "Basic Entrepreneurship Development Seminar/Training." The participants, who were mostly fishermen with no background in entrepreneurship, learned how to handle the livelihood grants they recently received including how to improve their livelihood procedures, boosting the efficiency and sustainability of their businesses and, eventually, their capacity to generate revenue.

As a new organization, they will learn how to handle business, and how they can grow the additional capital that the DOLE and the seven (7) associations that were selected as beneficiaries of fiberglass boats and fishing supplies.

The Public Employment Service Office (PESO) and Municipal Agriculture’s Office conducted activities and provided documentation. The associations signed a contract named "Katunayan ng Pagtanggap at Pagsang-Ayon sa mga Alituntunin". The contract enumerates the materials/supplies and the boat awarded to them as part of the livelihood program of DOLE. In this very contract, the responsibilities of beneficiaries were elaborately discussed, and every clause provided is voluntarily and willfully agreed upon by them. It was attested by the personnel from the Office of the Municipal Agriculturist.

For Calendar Year 2024, the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) issued SB Resolution No. 2024-060 dated April 30, 2024, which provides the Municipal Mayor with the authority to enter into an agreement for the DOLE Integrated Livelihood and Emergency Employment Program (DILEEP) projects in the municipality.

The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) and the Municipal Agriculturist have commenced operations at the reforestation site in Aryada, Alangan. Their activities include tree inventory, fertilization, and grass cutting, with comprehensive inventories conducted every six months. As of July 2024, the project reports a survival rate of 360 forest trees and 120 fruit trees.

The OIC-MPDC and MEO are already compliant with the recommendation. Copies of documentation for the delayed delivery of two (2) units of elevator installed in the PAGCOR Multipurpose Evacuation Center were submitted through email last May 30, 2024 and the hardcopies were also submitted on October 29, 2024.

Improvements in the documentation for infrastructure projects have been observed compared from past years; however, copies of contract agreements and procurement documents were not submitted on time.

update the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan incorporating the COVID-19 waste management plan; and
in coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan, to pass an ordinance banning the use of unnecessary single-use plastics at the Municipality including households, institutions, industrial and commercial establishments and other waste reduction ordinances.
MENRO to –
ensure the implementation of project components as proposed and submitted to DENR – EMB and compliance with the conditions on environmental management, general and specific conditions as well as restrictions as provided in the Environment Compliance Certificate; and
secure recommendation from DENR – EMB regarding the proper treatment of solid wastes contained in the closed dumpsite;
improve the construction of its sanitation vault in accordance with HCWM.
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that MENRO in coordination with MDRRMO to update the (i) Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) including the survey, mapping and planning to determine updated status of the forest cover of the Municipality; and (ii) include projects, programs and activities like tree planting and other PPAs which shall conserve the municipality’s forestry.

We recommended that Management, thru its GFPS, to (i) monitor the implementation of Adolescent Health and Youth Development program and the establishment of Teen Information Centers/Corners to measure the effectivity of the program vis-à-vis high incidence of teenage pregnancy; (ii) include additional Organization-focused PPAs to address GAD issues within the organization and to encourage their participation; (iii) give priority to both organization-focused and client-focused activities instead of the latter only, so that its GFPS and employees will understand further the purpose of GAD and will be more equipped in identifying and resolving various gender issues; and (iv) include the DILG-MLGOO and CSO Representatives from the women’s organization and the academe in the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team and IP Representative for the GFPS ExeCom.
We also recommended that the GFPS with the assistance of the SB Member-Chairperson of Committee on Women and Family to spearhead the formulation of the Municipality’s GAD Code to customize or cater exactly the needs of its clienteles, officials and employees.

We commended the Municipal government in its continuous compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations on proper deductions and remittance of GSIS premiums and loans in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 8291 for the year under audit.
We recommended and the Management agreed, thru the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant, to exert all the possible efforts to reconcile the Municipality’s records with the GSIS to prevent from (i) any legal action by the latter; and (ii) jeopardizing the benefits of members/beneficiaries.

We commended the Management in its continuous compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations on proper deduction and remittance of HDMF contributions in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679. We recommended and the Management agreed through the Municipal Accountant to extend effort in reconciling the balance with corresponding government office and proceed to procedures necessary to fully remit the balances.

We recommended and the Management agreed to coordinate with PhilHealth regarding the deficiencies reflected in Statement of Premium Account (SPA) totaling to ₱454,470.90 to prevent penalties/ future losses.

We recommended and the Management agreed to a) continue its compliance with laws, accounting, budgeting and auditing rules and regulations and act swiftly on noted deviations in audit to avoid audit suspensions and disallowances; and b) take appropriate action geared towards the settlement of the disallowance which are deemed final and executory in accordance with COA Revised Rules in Settlement of Accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to strictly withhold taxes on government purchases; (b) General Services Officer to issue PAR on purchased aircon and CCTV camera; and (c) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman to register in the GPPB Online Portal and post the information relative to the procurement projects undertaken through Emergency Procurement by the Municipality under the Bayanihan Act.

We recommended and the Management agreed to (a) post in the LGU official websites and social media accounts the list of beneficiaries who already received the emergency subsidy in accordance with the requirements of the JMC No. 2 series of 2020; (b) instruct the (i) Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the liquidation of the cash advance for the Social Amelioration Cash Assistance (LGU Counterpart); and (ii) Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer (a) enter into data sharing agreements with DSWD for the database on various social protections programs; and (b) refund the amounts on the duplicate claims for local SAP.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, as the pandemic situation improves, to strictly adhere to the rules and regulations in disbursement of funds of the Municipality; (b) Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of the supporting documentations before certifying on the completeness and propriety of the transactions; and (c) OIC-GSO to ensure the proper documentation of the distributed supplies and materials for proper accountability.

We recommended that the Management require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer (i) prepare and submit the duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year; and (ii) generate and submit to the Municipal Accounting Office the Certified List of Delinquent Accounts at the end of the year.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and the Management agreed to (a) require the (i) Municipal Accountant to (a) course all regularly procured supplies and materials to the appropriate inventory accounts following the perpetual inventory method; and (b) provide SLCs for supplies and materials by type/class; and (ii) OIC-GSO (a) to provide SCs for supplies and materials by type/class; (b) prepare and submit the  SSMI based on consolidated Acquisition and Issue Slip to the Municipal Accountant strictly on a weekly basis; and (c) conduct semestral count of inventories and submit RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; and (iii) Municipal Information Systems Officer study the possibility of procuring and or designing a database system for social protection beneficiaries of the Municipality and computerized inventory system to help in the control and recording of the receipt and distribution of inventory items.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) OIC-GSO (i) submit the duly reconciled RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office within the prescribed period; and (ii) maintain PCs by class and/or type and reconcile the balances with the Accounting records; and (b) Municipal Accountant maintain PPELCs by class and/or type to monitor and reconcile the balances with the PCs.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant check the completeness of the supporting documentations before certifying on the completeness and propriety of the transactions; and (b) Municipal Engineer and BAC Chairman ensure that the complete documentary requirements are attached to all infrastructure projects as required by COA Circular 2012-001 and submit the said documents to the Auditor’s Office for auditorial review and technical evaluation.

We recommended that Management:
organize an ICT Committee; and
draft, approve and implement an ISSP that will fast track government digitization.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct:
Municipal Human Resource Officer, guided by DA-DBM-CSC-DILG JMC No. 2 Series of 2020, study and propose the creation of ABEU; and
Municipal Agriculture Officer implement more agriculture and fisheries programs to ensure food security.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to GSIS accounts to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments therein; and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to (i) withhold premiums and remit the same to the GSIS within 10 calendar days following the month the contributions apply; and (ii) refund/collect over/under premiums deducted from employees as a result of reconciliation.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile the Due to BIR accounts to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments therein; and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer, as result of the reconciliation, withhold taxes and/or remit/refund the same from/to employees and BIR, respectively.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to Pag-IBIG account to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to withhold and remit the same from employees and Pag-IBIG, respectively.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to PhilHealth account to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments therein; and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to withhold and remit the same from employees and PhilHealth, respectively.

observations.recommendation: 1.5 	We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (a) monitor closely the recording of transactions and preparation of reports by the Treasury personnel to minimize, if not avoid, the occurrence of errors; (b) update and balance the cash book balances daily; and (c) trace, verify and reconcile regularly the cash book balances against the ledger balances maintained by the Accounting Department.

2.9 	We recommended that the Management require the (a) OIC-Municipal Treasurer (i) prepare and submit the duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year; and (ii) generate and submit to the Municipal Accounting Office the Certified List of Delinquent Accounts at the end of the year; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) reconcile the discrepancy between the RPT Receivable, Deferred Real Property Tax and SET Receivable and make the necessary adjustments thereof; and (ii) provide allowance for impairment for RPT and SET Receivable based on Certified List of Delinquent Accounts.

3.5 	We recommended that the Management instruct the:
(a) Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to all concerned officials and employees to enforce settlement of the unliquidated cash advances;
(b) Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to (i) adhere strictly with the governing laws, rules and regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances; and (ii) strictly monitor the status of cash advances for timely liquidation; and
(c) Impose sanctions on Accountable Officers who unjustly failed to liquidate their cash advances.

4.10 	We reiterated prior year’s recommendations that the Management require the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) course all regularly procured supplies and materials to the appropriate inventory accounts following the perpetual inventory method; and (ii) provide and maintain an updated SLCs for supplies and materials by type/class; and (b) OIC-GSO (i) prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant strictly on a weekly basis the  SSMI based on consolidated RIS; and (ii) conduct semestral count of inventories and submit RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

5.7 	We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that the Management instruct the (a) OIC-GSO (i) submit the duly reconciled RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office within the prescribed period; and (ii) maintain PCs by class and/or type and reconcile the balances with the Accounting records; and (b) Municipal Accountant maintain PPELCs by class and/or type to monitor and reconcile the balances with the PCs.

6.14 	We reiterated our recommendations that Management (a) update and upgrade the accounting system to facilitate the accurate and real-time financial reporting; (b) strictly comply with the timely submission of reports to the Office of the Auditor so as not to delay the verification and post audit activities; and (c) impose sanctions against the accountable officers who continuously failed to abide with the timely submission of documents and reports.

7.6 	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive in coordination with the Local Development Council (LDC) fast track the identification of priority developmental PPAs in the formulation of the Annual Investment Plan and optimize the utilization of the 20% DF to contribute to the attainment of desirable development outcomes of the Municipality.

8.10 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) LDRRMO (i) implement and monitor the planned PPAs in the LDRRMIP to increase the utilization rate of the 70% Mitigation Fund; (ii) revise the LDRRM Plan to include programs, projects and activities for the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the special trust fund; and (iii) prepare and submit a certified monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day of the following month; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) transfer all the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund; and (ii) transfer the separate bank account of LDRRMF in the trust fund books.

9.5 	We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive establish and strengthen a M&E Team thru issuance of EO or AO creating the M&E Team; and (ii) instruct the GFPS TWG to establish GAD database and processes provided by the guidelines to accelerate gender mainstreaming within the locality and ensure that all issues and concerns will be addressed in pursuit of women-empowerment and gender equality within the locality.

10.11 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a.   Instruct the MENRO Coordinator to coordinate closely with DENR-EMB Region III to facilitate approval of the Municipality’s SWMP;
b.	Upon securing the approved SWMP, instruct the Solid Waste Management Implementing Group to:
i.    prioritize the rehabilitation of open dumpsite,
ii.  fast track the construction and operation of the planned Municipal Solid Waste Processing Plant (MSWPP) and Central MRF, and
iii. strictly enforce mandatory segregation of wastes at source and the no segregation/no collection policy.

11.9 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Social Welfare and Development to prioritize the implementation of the projects funded thru the Bottom-up Budgeting (BuB) program in consonance with DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015.

12.5 	We recommended that the Former Municipal Mayor (a) submit the supporting documents for the cash advance granted for confidential fund totaling ₱19,000,000.00 to the Audit Team for post-audit; and (b) submit the liquidation of the confidential fund granted to the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU) for proper audit thereof.

13.7 	We recommended that the Management (a) stop charging expenses which are not among the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF as provided for in DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2017 and strictly adhere to the applicable laws, rules and regulations in utilizing the fund; (b) require the accountable and liable officials to return the disbursed fund amounting to ₱11,666,000.00; and (c) submit the supporting payroll or refund the amount of ₱116,000.00 for proper liquidation thereof.

14.8 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) instruct the BAC and/or the implementing departments to furnish the Auditor’s Office with copies of perfected contracts, purchase orders together with all the required supporting documents and the report of all on-going and to be implemented government PPAs in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2013-004; and
(b) require the officials and employees to attend trainings/seminars for them to enhance and update their knowledge and awareness on laws, rules and regulations.

15.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to GSIS account to identify any under or over remittance and take the necessary adjustments thereof; and (b) OIC-Municipal Treasurer (i) withhold and remit the total amount due to the GSIS within the 10th day of the following month; and (ii) refund/collect over/under premiums deducted from employees as a result of reconciliation.

16.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile the Due to BIR account and take the necessary adjustments; and (b) OIC-Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the remaining unsettled balance based on the result of the reconciliation.

17.4 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to Pag-IBIG account to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to withhold and remit the same from employees and Pag-IBIG, respectively.

18.5 	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Due to PhilHealth account to identify any over or under remittance and take the necessary adjustments and (b) the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to withhold and remit the same from employees and PhilHealth, respectively.

19.5 	We recommended that the persons liable for the disallowances be required to settle their liability and/or appeal to the appropriate tribunal within the prescribed period as stated in COA Circular No. 2009-006 should the appeal be dismissed by the COA Central Office.

observations.managementAction: Management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and require the BAC and/or the implementing departments to furnish the Auditor’s Office with copies of approved contracts and purchase orders within five days upon execution or issuance, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) LCE create an appraisal committee that will revalue/appraise properties with no costs; (b) OIC-GSO (i) submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office within the prescribed period; and (ii) maintain PCs by class and/or type and reconcile the balances with the Accounting records; and (c) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office maintain PPELCs by class and/or type to monitor and reconcile the balances with the PCs.

We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office exert all efforts to reconcile the discrepancy between the RPT and SET Receivables and take the necessary adjustments thereof; and (b) OIC-Municipal Treasurer (i) coordinate with the Municipal Assessor to update and generate the duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year; and (ii) submit to the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office the said list for the proper set-up of RPT and SET Receivables account at the beginning of each year.

We recommended that the (a) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer reconcile their books of accounts at least quarterly to minimize, if not avoid, the occurrence of errors; and (b) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office furnish the Audit Team of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the adjustments made on the reconciling items noted in the audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office to (a) verify the validity of outstanding receivable accounts and adjust the balances, if warranted; (b) take appropriate action/demand collection of the outstanding receivables before considering them for write-off; (c) submit to the Audit Team the proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect receivables as basis of the latter for the issuance of the final demand letter pursuant to COA Memorandum No. 2017-010 dated May 15, 2017; (d) submit to the Auditor’s Office the detailed schedule of receivables immediately.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office (a) compute/determine the impairment of Receivables and PPE in accordance with the PPSAS; (b) depreciate the School Buildings, Books and Other Land Improvements over their estimated useful life; and (c) recognize the impairment and depreciation in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office (a) transfer the funds received from different source agencies to the Trust Fund account of the Municipality and record all the transactions pertaining thereto; (b) maintain subsidiary ledgers for each fund transfer for monitoring of collection, utilization and liquidation of the funds; and (c) submit to the Auditor’s Office copies of approved MOA and/or Project Proposal for all fund transfers to verify if such funds were used only for the intended purpose/s.

We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) Local Chief Executive require the Local Development Council (LDC) to optimize the utilization of the 20% DF; and (b) LDC, thru the MPDO, fast track the identification of specific PPAs under the 20% DF in the formulation of the AIP and fully implement the identified PPAs thereof.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor fill up the mandatory positions in the MDRRM Office; (b) LDRRMO (i) implement and monitor the planned PPAs in the LDRRMIP; and (ii) prepare and submit a certified monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day of the following month; and (c) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at the end of the year.
We recommended that the Municipality stop charging expenses which are not among the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF as provided for in DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer (i) cause the payment through checks of all disbursements except payments in small amounts, as specified in Paragraphs 2.2 and 2.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and (ii) always adhere to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 on all the procurements of the Municipality.
We recommended that the Management refrain from granting allowances, incentives and fringe benefits in whatever form without legal basis/authority.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to (i) ascertain completeness of the supporting documents and ensure that claims are valid, proper and legal before authorizing and effecting payment to prevent improper and fraudulent use of government funds; and (ii) submit immediately to the Auditor’s Office the supporting documents of the above-cited financial assistance for review and verification of the transactions.

We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) OIC-Municipal Engineer and BAC Chairman make sure that the complete documentary requirements are attached to all infrastructure projects as required by various applicable laws, rules and regulations and submit the said documents to the Auditor’s Office for auditorial review and technical evaluation; and (b) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office (i) ensure that all original documents bear the signature of the Municipal Mayor; and (ii) prepare a checklist of the required documents and validate the completeness of the same before payments are processed.

We commended the Management for the continuous effort in implementing RA No. 9003 especially the construction of its own Sanitary Landfill. We recommended that continuous effort still be exercised to (i) speed up the construction of the planned Municipal Solid Waste Processing Plant (MSWPP); and (ii) prioritize and implement the approved Ten-Year SWMP in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9003.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, thru the GFPS TWG to (i) strengthen their GAD Focal Point System; (ii) formulate its own GAD Code aligned with the adopted Provincial GAD Code and suitable to the Municipality; (iii) establish GAD database and processes provided by the guidelines to accelerate gender mainstreaming within the locality and ensure that all issues and concerns will be addressed in pursuit of women-empowerment and gender equal locality; and (iv) prepare and submit the GAD AR to the Auditor’s Office within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to (a) withhold and remit the total amount due to the GSIS within the 10th day of the following month; and (b) coordinate with the GSIS to correct the unmatched amount withheld and take the necessary adjustments thereof.
We recommended that OIC-Municipal Accounting Office recognize and remit the taxes withheld within the reglementary period.
We commended the Municipal Mayor, the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer for their continuous effort to withhold and remit the amount due to the Pag-IBIG.

We recommended that the persons liable for the disallowances be required to settle their liability and/or appeal to the appropriate tribunal within the prescribed period as stated in COA Circular No. 2009-006 should the motion for reconsideration, petition for review and appeal be dismissed by the Supreme Court, Commission Proper and Regional Office No. III, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to designate other officers to handle cash advances to reduce the probability of error and irregularities; and (b) instruct the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to (i) ensure the completeness of the supporting documents and authorization for cash advances drawn and liquidated before the same are processed; (ii) monitor the status of cash advances and send reminders to Accountable Officers for the timely liquidation of their accountability; (iii) recognize cash advances for special purposes as Advances to Officers and Employees; and (iv) recommend for the imposition of sanctions on Accountable Officers who unjustly failed to liquidate their cash advances; and (c) Municipal Budget Officer formulate plans, programs and projects intended to address the long-term general welfare and concerns of senior citizens.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office adhere to the provisions of the COA, DBM, DILG, Government Commission for GOCCs and DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 and submit all cash advances granted for confidential fund together with the supporting documents to the concerned Audit Team for post-audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) endorse to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) the amendment on the resolution for a well-defined policy/guidelines incorporating the range of amount to be given based on various factors for Medical Assistance and Financial Assistance to Individual and Teams/Groups; (b) direct the Accounting and MSWDO to review requests thoroughly and examine the supporting documents before certifying on the disbursement vouchers to ensure that public funds are utilized only for public purposes and for the welfare of only qualified beneficiaries; and (c) direct the Treasury and MSWDO to properly monitor the loan accounts and enforce payment for the past due accounts.

We recommended that the (a) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer ascertain completeness of the supporting documents and signatures in every transaction before the same is processed, approved and paid; (b) Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairman ensure that all procurements are properly supported with documents; and (c) OIC-GSO issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment for every equipment purchased by the Municipality for the proper identification of accountability and responsibility of end-users.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that Management adopt measures in finding ways to run and manage the LPC other than charging its expenses to the SEF to ensure maximum benefits to primary and secondary schools as mandated by Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and Joint Circulars issued by DepEd, DBM and DILG.

We recommended that the (a) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office (i) ensure the completeness of required supporting documents and approval of proper authorities in every transactions before effecting payment; and (ii) transfer the BuB Funds to the Trust Fund account of the Municipality and record all the transactions pertaining thereto;  (b) Bids and Awards Committee ensure that procurement done thru competitive bidding bear all the required documents; and (c) General Services Officer issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment for every equipment purchased and Inventory Custodian Slip for every small tangible items procured for the proper identification of accountability and responsibility of end-users.

We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Local Development Council, optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund on PPAs outlined under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1; (b) Local Development Council identify specific PPAs in the formulation of the Annual Investment Plan specifically for the 20% development fund projects; (c) Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer always supervise and monitor the status of PPAs implementation and create feasible timetable to evade delays in implementing the development projects; and (d) OIC – Municipal Accounting Office maintain special accounts under the General Fund for development projects.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor position additional competent personnel in the Accounting Department that will help the OIC-MAO and her staff with the voluminous transactions of the Municipality; and (b) OIC-MAO (i) submit to the Auditor’s Office all accounts, financial records and required reports within the prescribed period; and (ii) strictly comply with the provisions of Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA, Sections 23 (1) and (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and other laws, rules and regulations on submission and rendering of accounts to avoid suspension and eventually disallowance of the Municipality’s transactions.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor assign additional personnel at Engineering Office; (b) Municipal Engineer/Bids and Awards Committee Chairman ensure that infrastructure projects bear all the required documents/engineering requirements and standards as required by various laws, rules and regulations; and (c) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office ensure the completeness of required supporting documents in every transaction before effecting payment.

We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive (i) utilize fully the yearly 70% Mitigation Fund of the LDRRMF for the programs, project and activities identified for disaster preparedness, prevention and mitigation pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-73 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and (ii) open a separate bank account for the unexpended LDRRMF balance; (b) OIC-Municipal Accounting Office (i) adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines of the LDRRMF to properly account the expenses related thereto; (ii) make the necessary adjustments for the recognition of disaster response and rescue equipment in the general fund which was charged to the trust fund; (iii) transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at the end of the year; and (iv) ascertain completeness of the supporting documents, information and signatures before the payments are processed; and (c) OIC-MDRRMO (i) prepare a certified monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit the report  on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and (ii) review and update annually the LDRRMFIP to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years.

We commended the Management for taking significant action and prioritizing the implementation of Solid Waste Management PPAs to fully comply with RA No. 9003. We recommended that extra effort still be exerted in prioritizing and implementing the approved Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and other environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment.
We also recommended that Management continue to involve the barangay officials in the strict implementation of the existing municipal solid waste management ordinances and environmental laws.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the GFPS, establish GAD Code, GAD Database and processes provided by the guidelines and strengthen the GFPS to accelerate gender mainstreaming within the locality and ensure that all issues and concerns will be addressed in pursuit of women-empowerment and gender equal locality. We also recommend that Management strictly expend the GAD funds in accordance with the approved Annual GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the (a) SB revisit the Municipal Ordinance to clarify provisions such as, but not limited to, the fines and penalties imposed; (b) LTFRB (i) strictly implement the Municipal Ordinance on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyances along the Roman Super-highway within the locality to prevent undesirable consequences which might endanger both pedestrians and motorists; (ii) impose fines and penalties on tricycles operating their routes on inner lanes of national highways; and (iii) continuously conduct information campaign about the provision of operating conditions for tricycles; and (c) Head of Limay Command Center submit the requested information by the Audit Team needed for evaluation such as number and location of existing CCTVs installed within the Municipality and sample data captured by the CCTVs.
We recommended the Municipal Mayor, the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office and OIC-Municipal Treasurer continue their effort to withhold and remit amount due to the GSIS.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accounting Office continue the effort of deducting the corresponding taxes from compensation of their officials and employees and all payments to suppliers/contractors and remitting the same within the prescribed period.
We recommended that the Municipality settle their audit disallowances within the prescribed period as stated in COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We reiterated prior year’s recommendations that Management comply strictly with the mandates of COA Circular No. 97-002. Cash disbursing officers should always liquidate the cash advances for payroll fund within five days after the end of each pay period. Always grant cash advance to the Cash Disbursing Officer for the net amount of payroll for a pay period and designate other officers to handle special cash advances, if possible.
